jokes.text: Having run out of fresh, exciting, new ideas to bring you myself
jokes.setup: Larry delivers what sounds like a heartfelt farewell speech
jokes.punchline: Admits he's quitting because he's out of ideas
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry Sanders self-awareness disguised as sincerity. The formal, flowery language makes the admission even funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Having run out of fresh, exciting, new ideas to bring you myself

jokes.text: Some day I may reenter the lists with a new saber, neither broken nor bent, and plow up the field all over again
jokes.setup: Continuing the farewell speech with increasingly grandiose language
jokes.punchline: The overwrought medieval metaphor becomes completely ridiculous
jokes.explanation: Larry's tendency toward pretentious language taken to absurd extremes. A bit wordy but captures his character perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Some day I may reenter the lists with a new saber, neither broken nor bent, and plow up the field all over again

jokes.text: Come on, blanket. We're going to go home
jokes.setup: After Larry's grandiose speech
jokes.punchline: Someone's mundane interaction with their blanket deflates the moment
jokes.explanation: Nice contrast between the formal speech and casual blanket comment. Good deflation moment.

dialogue.line: Come on, blanket. We're going to go home

jokes.text: You think I should mention God? Well, hell, we plugged everybody else on the planet. Let's give the deity his due
jokes.setup: Larry considering adding God to his farewell speech
jokes.punchline: Treating God like just another person who needs a plug
jokes.explanation: Excellent showbiz cynicism - reducing God to networking. 'Give the deity his due' is perfectly crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You think I should mention God? Well, hell, we plugged everybody else on the planet. Let's give the deity his due

jokes.text: The last 10 years have been your glorious gift to the people of this country. But they've also have been a gift... to a man by the name of Hank Kingsley
jokes.setup: Hank practicing heartfelt thanks about Larry's gift to America
jokes.punchline: Immediately pivots to make it about himself with the formal 'man by the name of'
jokes.explanation: Classic Hank - starts noble, immediately makes it about himself. The formal phrasing makes his narcissism even funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: The last 10 years have been your glorious gift to the people of this country. But they've also have been a gift... to a man by the name of Hank Kingsley

jokes.text: I think the hormones from the baby... You should've seen me at the end of Titanic. You think those people in the water were wailing?
jokes.setup: Beverly crying, someone attributing it to pregnancy hormones
jokes.punchline: Beverly compares her movie crying to drowning Titanic passengers
jokes.explanation: Dark but effective escalation. Beverly comparing her emotional response to actual tragedy is perfectly inappropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: I think the hormones from the baby... You should've seen me at the end of Titanic. You think those people in the water were wailing?

jokes.text: [Sighs] Fucking bunch of bullshit
jokes.setup: All the emotional farewell preparation
jokes.punchline: Someone's blunt dismissal of the sentiment
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective deflation of the sentimental buildup. Good timing and economy.

dialogue.line: [Sighs] Fucking bunch of bullshit

jokes.text: I have this buddy Ray... I could take the letters that just spell his name, and then I could put them on the lawn of his house
jokes.setup: Phil asking to take letters from the Larry sign
jokes.punchline: His elaborate plan to spell out 'Ray' on someone's lawn
jokes.explanation: Perfect low-level employee thinking. The specificity of the Ray plan makes it genuinely funny.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: I have this buddy Ray... I could take the letters that just spell his name, and then I could put them on the lawn of his house

jokes.text: But if it was up to me, seriously, you would not get them
jokes.setup: Larry explaining the letters are network property
jokes.punchline: His unnecessarily cruel addition that he wouldn't give them anyway
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry pettiness. The 'seriously' makes his meanness even funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: But if it was up to me, seriously, you would not get them

jokes.text: You know tomorrow night after the show? You're fired. But for the last time, so celebrate
jokes.setup: Phil complaining about the letters going in a dumpster
jokes.punchline: Larry firing him but framing it as a celebration
jokes.explanation: Masterful cruel comedy. The twisted logic of 'celebrate' being fired makes it brilliant.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You know tomorrow night after the show? You're fired. But for the last time, so celebrate

jokes.text: Don't ask me for the couch
jokes.setup: Phil just asked for letters
jokes.punchline: Larry preemptively shutting down the next request
jokes.explanation: Great efficiency. Shows Larry knows exactly how these conversations go.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Don't ask me for the couch

jokes.text: I think my thank-you should not come directly after yours, 'cause that would sound tit for tat
jokes.setup: Hank planning his thank-you speech timing
jokes.punchline: Overthinking the choreography of gratitude
jokes.explanation: Classic Hank neurosis. The phrase 'tit for tat' applied to thank-yous is perfectly Hank.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I think my thank-you should not come directly after yours, 'cause that would sound tit for tat

jokes.text: Don't lump me in with those fucking craft service retards
jokes.setup: Hank demanding a separate thank-you
jokes.punchline: His casual cruelty toward other staff
jokes.explanation: Shows Hank's hierarchy obsession and casual cruelty, though the language is dated/problematic.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Don't lump me in with those fucking craft service retards

jokes.text: Like wedding vows... I think you should wear a veil. I think we should come down the aisle together, maybe in white
jokes.setup: Hank comparing their thank-yous to wedding vows
jokes.punchline: Larry taking the metaphor literally and making it gay
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of Hank's metaphor into absurdity. Larry's deadpan delivery would make this land perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Like wedding vows... I think you should wear a veil. I think we should come down the aisle together, maybe in white

jokes.text: Think she'll cry? Well, I didn't go so far as to make the request, but that call can be made
jokes.setup: Larry asking if Carol Burnett will cry on the show
jokes.punchline: Artie treating manufactured emotion as a booking option
jokes.explanation: Brilliant showbiz cynicism. The idea that celebrity tears can be arranged is perfectly dark and specific to this world.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Think she'll cry?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't go so far as to make the request, but that call can be made

jokes.text: That's a man. That's a fucking man! I don't want a fucking man singing to me
jokes.setup: Learning Clint Black is booked to sing goodbye
jokes.punchline: Larry's panicked rejection of male serenade
jokes.explanation: Pure Larry neurosis about masculinity and public perception. His panic is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: That's a man. That's a fucking man! I don't want a fucking man singing to me

jokes.text: You don't have to worry about the homoerotic underpinnings. He's a country music singer
jokes.setup: Larry worried about a man singing to him
jokes.punchline: Artie's logic that country music cancels homoeroticism
jokes.explanation: Artie's twisted logic is perfect. The phrase 'homoerotic underpinnings' is brilliantly specific and academic.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You don't have to worry about the homoerotic underpinnings. He's a country music singer

jokes.text: Well, that's iffy because we never let him plug Ace Ventura. Come on, everyone thought he was too broad
jokes.setup: Trying to book Jim Carrey for the final show
jokes.punchline: They rejected him when he was unknown for being 'too broad'
jokes.explanation: Good industry satire about shortsighted booking decisions. The irony of rejecting a future superstar is well-crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, that's iffy because we never let him plug Ace Ventura. Come on, everyone thought he was too broad

jokes.text: Hey, I think that's Warren Beatty in the parking lot. He must still be putting the finishing touches on Bulworth. That's already out. Well, the man is never satisfied
jokes.setup: Seeing Warren Beatty, assuming he's working on his movie
jokes.punchline: Learning the movie's already out but rationalizing it anyway
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment showing Hollywood people's rationalization skills. Artie's recovery is smooth and believable.

dialogue.speaker: Norman
dialogue.line: Hey, I think that's Warren Beatty in the parking lot. He must still be putting the finishing touches on Bulworth.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: That's already out.

dialogue.speaker: Norman
dialogue.line: Well, the man is never satisfied

jokes.text: I could say goodbye to you now... There are no cameras or anything here. Tomorrow night, millions of people
jokes.setup: Warren offering to say goodbye now
jokes.punchline: Larry explaining the obvious difference between private and televised goodbye
jokes.explanation: Good example of Larry's entertainment industry mindset. The contrast between private and public gestures is well-drawn.

dialogue.speaker: Warren
dialogue.line: I could say goodbye to you now... There are no cameras or anything here.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Tomorrow night, millions of people

jokes.text: We believe that David's in love with Larry... He's married. So was Rock Hudson, ok? Oh, it wasn't real. It was arranged by the studios
jokes.setup: Discussion of David Duchovny being 'too needy' for the show
jokes.punchline: Escalating from love to marriage conspiracy theories
jokes.explanation: Good escalation into Hollywood conspiracy territory. The Rock Hudson reference adds historical specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: We believe that David's in love with Larry...

dialogue.speaker: Norman
dialogue.line: He's married.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: So was Rock Hudson, ok? Oh, it wasn't real. It was arranged by the studios

jokes.text: He slipped on some ice making his new movie. The whole thing was all on ice or something. I don't know
jokes.setup: Explaining why Kevin Costner dropped out
jokes.punchline: Artie's vague, dismissive explanation of the ice movie
jokes.explanation: Artie's casual dismissal of details is in character but not particularly sharp.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: He slipped on some ice making his new movie. The whole thing was all on ice or something. I don't know

jokes.text: I know his number by heart because he's a good friend of mine
jokes.setup: Larry about to call David Duchovny
jokes.punchline: The immediate awkwardness of the actual call contradicts his claim
jokes.explanation: Sets up the awkward phone call perfectly. Larry's self-delusion about friendship is characteristic.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I know his number by heart because he's a good friend of mine

jokes.text: No, it's just that, uh, Téa's here... It's my mother. It's my mother
jokes.setup: David whispering on the phone with Larry
jokes.punchline: Lying to his wife about who's calling
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing the weird Larry-David dynamic. The mother lie is awkwardly specific.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, it's just that, uh, Téa's here... It's my mother. It's my mother

jokes.text: I'm dating Illeana Douglas, as a matter of fact. She's fantastic. It's just a wonderful male-female relationship, and I am so happy
jokes.setup: David asking if Larry is dating anyone
jokes.punchline: Larry's overly defensive, formal description of heterosexual dating
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry overcompensation. The phrase 'male-female relationship' is hilariously clinical and defensive.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I'm dating Illeana Douglas, as a matter of fact. She's fantastic. It's just a wonderful male-female relationship, and I am so happy

jokes.text: Meet me at the Bel-Air Hotel around noon... I am not meeting him at the Bel-Air. I am not meeting him...
jokes.setup: David suggesting a romantic hotel meeting
jokes.punchline: Larry's panicked repetition about not going there
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the awkward dynamic. Larry's panicked repetition shows his discomfort perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Meet me at the Bel-Air Hotel around noon...

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I am not meeting him at the Bel-Air. I am not meeting him...

jokes.text: Téa? Uh, she went golfing. Really? Yeah. Well, that's fantastic.
jokes.setup: Larry asking about David's wife
jokes.punchline: David's obvious lie and Larry's uncomfortable enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: The obvious lie about Téa and Larry's awkward response heighten the uncomfortable romantic subtext.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Téa? Uh, she went golfing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, that's fantastic.

jokes.text: His behavior was so detesticle... I meant despicable
jokes.setup: David commenting on someone's behavior
jokes.punchline: Accidentally combining 'despicable' and 'testicle'
jokes.explanation: Solid malapropism that creates an accidentally apt word. The correction makes it even funnier.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: His behavior was so detesticle... I meant despicable

jokes.text: Larry's a thing of the past. The guy's the 8-track. He's a fucking horse and buggy, that guy. This is Darwinism
jokes.setup: Artie talking to Jon Stewart about replacing Larry
jokes.punchline: Increasingly outdated metaphors for Larry's obsolescence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie ruthlessness. The escalating obsolescence metaphors are well-crafted and characteristic.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Larry's a thing of the past. The guy's the 8-track. He's a fucking horse and buggy, that guy. This is Darwinism

jokes.text: I would skip 'challenge.' Leave out 'challenge.' Do 'honor.' That's a good note, and I'll-- Tighten it up. Get to the jokes
jokes.setup: Jon planning his thank-you speech to Larry
jokes.punchline: Larry giving notes on his own tribute like it's a script
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta moment. Larry treating his own farewell like entertainment is perfect character work and show commentary.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I would skip 'challenge.' Leave out 'challenge.' Do 'honor.' That's a good note, and I'll-- Tighten it up. Get to the jokes

jokes.text: Did Stevie Grant tell you that he was signing me beforehand? No. No, he didn't. I didn't know that. Well-- Well, well, hello, Jon
jokes.setup: Larry asking Jon about advance knowledge of his replacement
jokes.punchline: Artie's awkward interruption when caught
jokes.explanation: Good awkward moment showing behind-the-scenes betrayal. Artie's forced cheer is perfectly uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Did Stevie Grant tell you that he was signing me beforehand?

dialogue.speaker: Jon
dialogue.line: No. No, he didn't. I didn't know that.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Well-- Well, well, hello, Jon

jokes.text: Faces from the past. They always show up for a wrapped gift
jokes.setup: Larry asking about all the people showing up
jokes.punchline: Artie's cynical assessment of farewell parasites
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie cynicism in very few words. 'Wrapped gift' is a brilliant metaphor for finale vultures.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Faces from the past. They always show up for a wrapped gift

jokes.text: I just wanted to see after you fired me and fucked up my life if you'd stand there and smile at me like we were old buddies. Well, now you know
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining why he came to the finale
jokes.punchline: Larry's casual confirmation that yes, he would fake friendship
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark moment. Larry's shameless admission of his superficiality is perfectly in character and brutal.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I just wanted to see after you fired me and fucked up my life if you'd stand there and smile at me like we were old buddies. Well, now you know

jokes.text: This is Martin, the sound guy you fired after he hit you in the head with a boom. 3 days in a row. Still a record
jokes.setup: Introduction of the fired sound guy
jokes.punchline: The absurd specificity of the boom mic record
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation. The pride in the 'record' transforms a firing into an achievement, perfectly absurd.

dialogue.line: This is Martin, the sound guy you fired after he hit you in the head with a boom. 3 days in a row. Still a record

jokes.text: I didn't realize it was the end of the show till last night. I was lying in bed with a woman, and I said, 'Seriously, we're gonna have you on soon.'
jokes.setup: Larry doing his opening monologue about the show ending
jokes.punchline: The awkward promise he made to a woman in bed
jokes.explanation: Good opening joke showing Larry's automatic showbiz mode even in intimate moments. Well-constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I didn't realize it was the end of the show till last night. I was lying in bed with a woman, and I said, 'Seriously, we're gonna have you on soon.'

jokes.text: This show is like my sex life in that it lasts about an hour, there's applause, and I'm usually interrupted by Hank
jokes.setup: Larry comparing the show to his sex life
jokes.punchline: The unexpected Hank interruption element
jokes.explanation: Excellent three-part structure with perfect escalation. The Hank element is unexpected and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: This show is like my sex life in that it lasts about an hour, there's applause, and I'm usually interrupted by Hank

jokes.text: When we started this show 10 years ago, Michael Jackson still looked like Michael Jackson
jokes.setup: Larry reflecting on how long the show has run
jokes.punchline: Michael Jackson's dramatic physical transformation as a time marker
jokes.explanation: Brilliant time passage joke. Uses a shocking cultural reference as a measuring stick, perfectly dark.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: When we started this show 10 years ago, Michael Jackson still looked like Michael Jackson

jokes.text: I'm acting now... It's a snap. One minute I'm talking to Tempestt Bledsoe at 1:35 in the morning about her dogs or something, next thing you know I got a nomination
jokes.setup: Greg explaining his transition from talk show to acting
jokes.punchline: The absurd ease of going from late-night hosting to Oscar nomination
jokes.explanation: Good industry satire about the randomness of success. The Tempestt Bledsoe specificity is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Greg Kinnear
dialogue.line: I'm acting now... It's a snap. One minute I'm talking to Tempestt Bledsoe at 1:35 in the morning about her dogs or something, next thing you know I got a nomination

jokes.text: I can't do comedy right now... You're leaving, Larry. Just when I thought everything was solid in my life... You decide to go
jokes.setup: Jim Carrey starting a comedy bit on the show
jokes.punchline: Breaking character to express real emotional neediness
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta moment - comedian unable to perform comedy about the show ending. Subverts expectations perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Jim Carrey
dialogue.line: I can't do comedy right now... You're leaving, Larry. Just when I thought everything was solid in my life... You decide to go

jokes.text: I can't get it up. Ever since the announcement, I've been flaccid
jokes.setup: Jim expressing his emotional distress about Larry leaving
jokes.punchline: Making it explicitly about sexual dysfunction
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the breakdown. 'Flaccid' is a perfect word choice for maximum discomfort.

dialogue.speaker: Jim Carrey
dialogue.line: I can't get it up. Ever since the announcement, I've been flaccid

jokes.text: The elaborate 'No Way' chant and song performance
jokes.setup: Jim getting the audience to chant about Larry staying
jokes.punchline: The completely over-the-top musical performance that follows
jokes.explanation: Massive escalation into complete absurdity. While long, it builds effectively to the screeching climax.

dialogue.speaker: Jim Carrey
dialogue.line: The elaborate 'No Way' chant and song performance

jokes.text: Let's just cut the crap, Larry. You never liked my work. Till I got hugely famous
jokes.setup: Larry thanking Jim for the performance
jokes.punchline: Jim's blunt assessment of their relationship
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback and brutal honesty. Cuts through all the fake sentiment with perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jim Carrey
dialogue.line: Let's just cut the crap, Larry. You never liked my work. Till I got hugely famous

jokes.text: Let's just cut the crap, Larry. Ok? You never liked my work. Huh? Till I got hugely famous.
jokes.setup: Larry thanking Jim after the performance
jokes.punchline: Jim's brutal honesty about their relationship
jokes.explanation: Devastating industry truth delivered with perfect directness, exposing Hollywood's shallow relationships.

dialogue.speaker: Jim Carrey
dialogue.line: Let's just cut the crap, Larry. Ok? You never liked my work. Huh? Till I got hugely famous.

jokes.text: I'm here for 3 good reasons. Last show, big ratings, movie coming out. Bim, bam, boom. Otherwise, I'd be sitting home watching Nightline
jokes.setup: Jim continuing his honest assessment
jokes.punchline: Reducing his appearance to pure business calculation
jokes.explanation: Devastating showbiz honesty. 'Bim, bam, boom' adds perfect casual dismissiveness to the calculation.

dialogue.speaker: Jim Carrey
dialogue.line: I'm here for 3 good reasons. Last show, big ratings, movie coming out. Bim, bam, boom. Otherwise, I'd be sitting home watching Nightline

jokes.text: For what, Talk Soup?
jokes.setup: Someone defending Greg Kinnear's Oscar nomination
jokes.punchline: Clint's dismissive confusion about what Greg could be nominated for
jokes.explanation: Good quick dismissal. Shows the country singer's disconnect from movie industry.

dialogue.speaker: Clint Black
dialogue.line: For what, Talk Soup?

jokes.text: I haven't seen such a rumble since the Stones played Altamont
jokes.setup: The backstage fight breaking out
jokes.punchline: Tom's rock star reference to a famously violent concert
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific reference. Tom Petty would naturally think of Altamont, and it escalates the fight appropriately.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Petty
dialogue.line: I haven't seen such a rumble since the Stones played Altamont

jokes.text: I haven't seen such a rumble since the Stones played Altamont.
jokes.setup: Fight breaking out between guests
jokes.punchline: Historical rock concert violence comparison
jokes.explanation: Good rock history reference that appropriately escalates the backstage chaos to legendary proportions.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Petty
dialogue.line: I haven't seen such a rumble since the Stones played Altamont.

jokes.text: You've already got Roy Rogers.
jokes.setup: Tom Petty being asked to stay and perform
jokes.punchline: Dismissing Clint Black as a dated country act
jokes.explanation: Sharp putdown with vintage reference, perfectly delivered with rock star disdain.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Petty
dialogue.line: You've already got Roy Rogers.
